content.full_text:
Re
Faln PollrrcAt. PRACTTcES C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 o 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95}12-0807
(916)322-s660. Fax (916) 322-0886
lune 19,2002
Leslie Cook, CMC
City of SantaCruz
809 Center Street, Room 9
Santa Cruz, CA 95060
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. l-02-123
Dear Ms. Cook:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the mass mailing
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 We are treatingyour request as one
for informal assistance because your request lacks facts necessary for formai advice and
you have not identified a specific oflicial on whose behalf you are requesting advice.2
QUESTIONS
1. When a council member sponsors a pioject, may you credit the council member
for the project on the Internet?
2. When a council member sponsors a project, may you credit the council member
for the project in a newsletter?
3. May the sponsoring council member be listed by phone number, address and/or
email in the newsletter?
4
May the council members be listed individually as having voted for or against the
project either on the Internet or.in any written publication produced by the City of
Santa Cruz?
I Govemment Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2,sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114;Regulation Section 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
<:


File No. I-02-123
Page No. 2
CONCLUSIONS
l. Yes, you may include information regarding project sponsorship by a council
member on the Internet. The mass mailing prohibition does not apply to
electronic mail or websites.
2. If the council member is "affrliated" with the agency producing the newsletter and
is not acting in "cooperation, consultatioq coordination, or concert" (regulation
1 8901(a)(2)(b) with the creators of the newsletteq then you may include
information regarding project sponsorship by a council member in the newsletter.
If the council member is "affiliated" with the agency producing the newsletter and
is acting in "cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert" with the creators
of the newsletter, then you may not include the council member's name.
3. Yes, the sponsoring council member may be listed by phone number, address
andlor email on the Internet. However, in a written publication produced by the
City of Santa Cruz there are certain limitations that are explained in detail below
4. Yes, the council members may be listed individually as having voted for or
against the project on the Internet. However, in a written publication produced by
the City of Santa Cruz there are certainlimitations that are explained in detail
below.
FACTS
Several years ago, the City of Santa Cruz requested an advice letter on the City of
Santa Cruz's newsletteq and whether council members could be credited with bylines.
The Commission responded that the use of council members' names was not permissible
in the publicly funded mass mailing.
With increasing usage of the Internet, the question has again come up. If a
council member sponsors a project, which is approved by the city council, can an
individual council member be given credit on the Internet and in any written publication
produced by the City of Santa Cruz?
If the answer is yes, then can the sponsoring council member be listed as a
telephone, letter and electronic mail contact?
If not, can the council members be listed individually as having voted for or
against the project, on the Internet and in any written publication produced by the City of
Santa Cruz?


File No. I-02-123
Page No. 3
ANALYSIS
Section 89001 provides that "[n]o newsletter or other mass mailing shall be sent
at public expense." Regulation 18901 (copy enclosed) interprets this provision. In
pertinent part, regulation 18901 provides four criteria for what is considered a "mass
mailing."
1. Delivery
(l) Any item sent is delivered,by any means, to the recipient at his or her
residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For purposes
of this subdivision (a)(l), the item delivered to the recipient must be a tangible
item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document."
(Regulation 18901(a)(l) ) (Emphasis added.)
I Internet: According to regulation 18901(a)(1), a publicly-funded mailing is a
prohibited mass mailing if it is delivered as a tangible item to the recipient at his or
her residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. Consistent with
regulation 18901, the ban is curently applicable to tangible items only. (Doyle
Advice Letter, No. I-94-364.) Since distribution over the Internet is not distribution
of a tangible item, Internet pages are not prohibited mass mailings under the Act.3
Since the Internet is not covered by the mass mailing regulation, we do not go on to
discuss Internet communications in the other factors.
t Newsletter: The newsletter would be a tangible item sent to people's residences
2. Inclusion of a Reference to an Elected Officer
"(2) The item sent either:
(A) Features an elected officer affrliated with the agency which produces or
sends the mailing, or
(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected
officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and
is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert
with the elected officer." (Regulation 18901(a)(2).)
1T fl
3 By way of historical background, "electronic mail communications" was included in regulation
18901 from March 12,1989 until December 13, 1989. This language was removed from the regulation.
(Conrmission minutes, December 12-13,1989; staff memorandum entifled "Adopt Regulation 18901 -
Mass Mailings Sent at Public Expense," December l, 1989.) On October 6,2000, inresponse to public
inqtriry and a CaIPERS request concerning re-examining of regulation 18901, the Commission adopted
stafFs proposal to "choose to await consideration of e-mails intlis context until a more comprehensive
discussion can be had, which considers the impact of the Internet and electronic communication on all
aspects of the Political Reform Act. (Commission minutes, October 6, 2000 staff memorandum entifled
"Approval of Regulation Calendar for the Year 2001," September 26,2000.)


File No. I-02-123
Page No. 4
"(l) 'Elected officer affiliated with the agency' means an
elected officer who is a member, officer, or employee of
the agency, or of a subunit thereof such as a committee, or
who has supervisory control over the agency, or who
appoints one or more members of the agency." (Regulation
18e01(c)(1) )
"Q)'Features an elected officer' means that the item
mailed includes the elected officer's photograph or
signature, or singles out the elected officer by the manner
of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the
document, such as by headlines, captions, type size,
typeface, or type color." (Regulation 18901 (cXz).)
t Newsletter: Where the elected officer initiates the mailing, exercises direction or
control over the content, production, or distribution of the document, regulation
18901(a)(2)(B) applies. (Kibbe-Chairs AdviceLetter, No. I-89-251.) we do not
have the information necessary to determine if these factors are present in this case
In a1l other cases, regulation 18901(a)(2)(A) applies. Under this standard, a
council member cannot be "featured" in the newsletter. Consequently, a city council
member may be referenced in an article in the city's newsletter, so long as the item
mailed does not include "the elected officer's photograph or signature, or singles out the
elected officer by the manner of display of his or her name or office in the layout of the
document, such as by headlines, captions, type size, typeface, or type color", including
use of a byline. (Regulation 18901(c)(2).)
3. Public Moneys
"(3) (A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or
(B) Costs of design, production, and printing.. is done with the intent of
sending the item other than as permitted by this regulation." (Regulation
18e01(a)(3) )
t Newsletter. The cost of the mailing appears to be paid with public funds. We are
assuming that is the case here.
4. Mass Mailings
"(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single
calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and
any item described in subdivision (b) [exceptions]." (Regulation 18901(aX4) )
t Newsletter: We are also assuming that more than 200 unsolicited copies of the
proposed mailing would be sent because that information was also not available.


File No. I-02-123
Page No. 5
Exceptions to Regulation 18901
If the mailing in question meets the definition of "mass mailing" for the pu{poses
of section 89001, the mass mailing is limited to 200 pieces per calendar month. Please
note, however, that where exceptions apply to specific items, these items need not be
counted in calculating the 200 item limit. For instance, if your agency sent out 200
newsletters featuring a council member who is "affrliated" with your agency> and your
agency also sends out 500 newsletters in which the elected officer's name only appears in
an agency roster, the 500 items will be counted separately. The 500 items sent out would
not be counted in the same category as the items that fall under the 200 limit because the
500 items fall under an exception to the regulation. Thus, neither set of publications will
be a "mass mailing" under section 89001.
Subsections (b)(t) and (b)(S) ofregulation 18901 provide relevant exceptions to
the mass mailing prohibition:
"(l) Any item in which the elected offrcer's name appears only in the
letterhead or logotype of the stationery, forms (including 'For Your
Information' or'Compliments of cards), and envelopes of the agency
sending the mailing, or of a committee of the ageficy , or of the elected
officer, or in a roster listing containing the names of all elected officers of
the agency. In any such item, the names of all elected officers must
appear in the same type size, typeface, type color, and location. Such item
may not include the elected officer's photograph, signature, or any other
reference to the elected officer, except as specifically permitted in this
subdivision (bxl) or elsewhere in this regulation. o
1T fl
"(8) A telephone directory, organization chart, or similar
listing or roster which includes the names of elected
officers as well as other individuals in the agency sending
the mailing, where the name of each elected officer and
individual listed appears in the same type size, typeface,
and type color. Such item may not include an elected
officer's photograph, name, signature, or any other
reference to an elected officer, except as specifically
permitted in this subdivision (b)(8) or elsewhere in this
regulation."
According to regulation 18901(b)(8), you may list the names of the council with a
telephone number to reach them or include an organizational chart with the same
information, if you otherwise comply with regulation 18901(bX8) You would be
allowed to include the telephone numbers, addresses andlor email addresses as a list or
roster.

3. Yes, the sponsoring council member may be listed by phone number, address

QUESTION: 1. When a council member sponsors a pioject, may you credit the council member
for the project on the Internet?
2. When a council member sponsors a project, may you credit the council member
for the project in a newsletter?
3. May the sponsoring council member be listed by phone number, address and/or
email in the newsletter?
4
May the council members be listed individually as having voted for or against the
project either on the Internet or.in any written publication produced by the City of
Santa Cruz?
2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Section 83114;Regulation Section 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)
<:

CONCLUSION: l. Yes, you may include information regarding project sponsorship by a council
member on the Internet. The mass mailing prohibition does not apply to
electronic mail or websites.
2. If the council member is "affrliated" with the agency producing the newsletter and
is not acting in "cooperation, consultatioq coordination, or concert" (regulation
1 8901(a)(2)(b) with the creators of the newsletteq then you may include
information regarding project sponsorship by a council member in the newsletter.
If the council member is "affiliated" with the agency producing the newsletter and
is acting in "cooperation, consultation, coordination, or concert" with the creators
of the newsletter, then you may not include the council member's name.
3. Yes, the sponsoring council member may be listed by phone number, address
andlor email on the Internet. However, in a written publication produced by the
City of Santa Cruz there are certain limitations that are explained in detail below
4. Yes, the council members may be listed individually as having voted for or
against the project on the Internet. However, in a written publication produced by
the City of Santa Cruz there are certainlimitations that are explained in detail
below.
